1:
"I say then, Hath God cast away his people? God forbid. For I also am an Israelite, of the seed of Abraham, of the tribe of Benjamin." The witnesses note the question of Israel's total rejection, denied. On "Hath God cast away his people?" the grammatical voices note the objection. Barnes and Robertson note Paul anticipates the inference from chs. 9-10: has God utterly and finally rejected his ancient people? He vehemently denies it ("God forbid," me genoito).

On "I also am an Israelite," the witnesses note the proof. Barnes and Robertson note Paul offers himself as a living proof: he, a Jew (of Abraham's seed, the tribe of Benjamin), is saved — so God has not cast away all Israel; a believing remnant exists.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the comfort: God's rejection of unbelieving Israel is not total; the apostle himself, and others, are pledges of the preserved remnant. The verse displays the question of Israel's rejection, denied by Paul's own case.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul anticipates the inference from chs. 9-10 — has God utterly rejected his ancient people? — and vehemently denies it ("God forbid," Barnes, Robertson), offering himself as living proof: he, a Jew (of Abraham's seed, the tribe of Benjamin), is saved, so God has not cast away all Israel; a believing remnant exists. Calvin and Theodoret note the rejection is not total, the apostle a pledge of the preserved remnant. The verse displays the question denied. The verse marks the objection: God cast away his people? The verse displays the denial: God forbid. The verse displays the proof: Paul himself an Israelite. The verse displays the remnant: a believing portion. The question denied displays that God has not utterly rejected Israel. The verse displays the vehemence: God forbid. The verse displays the evidence: Paul's own case. The verse displays the comfort: a remnant preserved. The question of Israel's total rejection, denied by Paul's own case, displays that God has not cast away his people: the existence of believing Jews — Paul himself foremost — proves that the rejection of unbelieving Israel is not total or final, a remnant being preserved by grace. The verse opens the chapter's argument that Israel's fall is neither total nor final, and grounds it in God's foreknowledge (v.2).
2:
"God hath not cast away his people which he foreknew. Wot ye not what the scripture saith of Elias? how he maketh intercession to God against Israel, saying," The witnesses note God's foreknown people and the example of Elijah. On "his people which he foreknew," the grammatical voices note the ground. Barnes and Robertson note God has not cast away the people he foreknew — "foreknew" denoting his prior knowledge and choice (as 8:29), the elect whom he had marked out.

On the example of Elijah, the witnesses note the parallel. Paul appeals to Elijah's day (1 Kings 19): when the prophet thought himself alone, complaining against Israel, God's answer (v.4) revealed a hidden remnant.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the lesson: as in Elijah's time, so now, when Israel seems wholly apostate, God preserves a remnant unseen — his foreknown people are never cast away. The verse displays God's foreknown people and the Elijah parallel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God has not cast away the people he foreknew — "foreknew" denoting his prior knowledge and choice (as 8:29), the elect marked out (Barnes, Robertson) — and Paul appeals to Elijah's day (1 Kings 19): when the prophet thought himself alone, complaining against Israel, God's answer would reveal a hidden remnant. Calvin and Theodoret note that, as then, so now God preserves a remnant unseen. The verse displays God's foreknown people and the Elijah parallel. The verse marks the ground: the foreknown people. The verse displays the example: Elijah. The verse displays the complaint: against Israel. The verse displays the lesson: a hidden remnant. God's foreknown people display the security of the elect against total rejection. The verse displays the foreknowledge: the elect marked out. The verse displays the parallel: Elijah's day. The verse displays the preservation: a remnant unseen. God's foreknown people, and the example of Elijah, display the security of the elect against total rejection: as in Elijah's time, when Israel seemed wholly apostate, God preserved a remnant, so now his foreknown people are never cast away. The verse displays the principle that God always preserves a remnant according to his foreknowledge, and recounts the prophet's complaint (v.3).
3:
"Lord, they have killed thy prophets, and digged down thine altars; and I am left alone, and they seek my life." The brief verse notes Elijah's complaint against Israel. The grammatical voices note the citation (1 Kings 19:10, 14). Barnes and Robertson note Elijah's despairing complaint: Israel had killed the prophets, torn down God's altars, and he alone was left, his life sought.

On the despair, the witnesses note the prophet's sense of isolation. Elijah believed himself the only faithful one remaining — the apostasy seemed total, the faithful all destroyed.

The eastern Theodoret notes the parallel with Paul's day: as Elijah lamented Israel's apostasy, so the Jews now seemed wholly fallen — yet a remnant remained. The verse displays Elijah's complaint of Israel's total apostasy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Elijah's despairing complaint (1 Kings 19:10, 14) — Israel had killed the prophets, torn down God's altars, and he alone was left, his life sought (Barnes, Robertson); the prophet believing himself the only faithful one remaining, the apostasy seeming total. Theodoret notes the parallel with Paul's day: as Elijah lamented, so the Jews now seemed wholly fallen, yet a remnant remained. The verse displays Elijah's complaint. The verse marks the citation: 1 Kings 19. The verse displays the complaint: prophets killed, altars cast down. The verse displays the isolation: I am left alone. The verse displays the parallel: with Paul's day. Elijah's complaint displays the seeming totality of Israel's apostasy. The verse displays the killing: of the prophets. The verse displays the despair: I alone am left. The verse displays the threat: my life sought. Elijah's complaint against Israel — they have killed thy prophets and I am left alone — displays the seeming totality of the nation's apostasy: the prophet, despairing, believed himself the sole survivor of the faithful. The verse displays the parallel with Paul's own day, when the Jews seemed wholly fallen, and sets up the divine answer that revealed a hidden remnant (v.4). The prophet stands here as the very type of the despairing believer who sees only the triumph of evil, that the divine answer might the more gloriously correct his too-narrow reckoning of the faithful.
4:
"But what saith the answer of God unto him? I have reserved to myself seven thousand men, who have not bowed the knee to the image of Baal." The witnesses note the divine answer: the reserved remnant. On "the answer of God," the grammatical voices note the response. Barnes and Robertson note God's reply to Elijah (1 Kings 19:18): the prophet was not alone — God had reserved seven thousand who had not bowed to Baal.

On "I have reserved to myself," the witnesses note the divine preservation. Barnes notes the emphatic "I have reserved" (kept for myself) — the remnant preserved by God's own act and grace, not by their own strength; the elect kept faithful.

The Reformed Calvin notes the doctrine: the seven thousand were preserved by God's election and grace, a proof that God always keeps a remnant for himself; their faithfulness owing to his keeping. The verse displays the divine answer: the reserved remnant of seven thousand.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God's reply to Elijah (1 Kings 19:18) — the prophet was not alone; God had reserved seven thousand who had not bowed to Baal (Barnes, Robertson); the emphatic "I have reserved" marking the remnant preserved by God's own act and grace, not their own strength. Calvin notes the seven thousand were preserved by God's election and grace, a proof that God always keeps a remnant. The verse displays the divine answer. The verse marks the response: the answer of God. The verse displays the reservation: seven thousand kept. The verse displays the preservation: by God's act. The verse displays the doctrine: the elect kept. The divine answer displays God's preservation of a remnant by grace. The verse displays the reply: not alone. The verse displays the number: seven thousand. The verse displays the keeping: by God himself. The divine answer — I have reserved to myself seven thousand who have not bowed to Baal — displays God's gracious preservation of a remnant: even when apostasy seemed total, God had kept for himself a faithful number, preserved by his own act and grace, not their strength. The verse displays the doctrine that God always reserves a remnant according to election, and applies it to the present time (v.5).
5:
"Even so then at this present time also there is a remnant according to the election of grace." The witnesses note the present remnant according to the election of grace. On "at this present time," the grammatical voices note the application. Barnes and Robertson note Paul applies the Elijah principle to his own day: just as then, so now, there is a remnant of believing Jews preserved.

On "according to the election of grace," the witnesses note the ground. Barnes, Gill, and Calvin note the remnant exists according to election — God's gracious choice; their salvation owing to grace, not merit, the believing Jews chosen by God's free favour.

The Reformed Calvin notes the doctrine: the remnant is preserved by the election of grace, not by their own works — God's free choice the source of their faith. The verse displays the present remnant according to the election of grace.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul applies the Elijah principle to his own day — just as then, so now there is a remnant of believing Jews preserved (Barnes, Robertson) — according to the election of grace, God's gracious choice, their salvation owing to grace not merit (Barnes, Gill, Calvin). The verse displays the present remnant. The verse marks the application: at this present time. The verse displays the remnant: believing Jews. The verse displays the ground: the election of grace. The verse displays the source: God's free favour. The present remnant displays the continuance of God's gracious election. The verse displays the parallel: as in Elijah's day. The verse displays the remnant: preserved now. The verse displays the grace: not merit. The present remnant according to the election of grace displays the continuance of God's gracious choice: as in Elijah's day, so now, a remnant of believing Jews is preserved, not by their own works but by God's free election. The verse displays that even amid Israel's general unbelief, grace preserves a chosen remnant, and presses the antithesis of grace and works (v.6). Thus the principle that held in the darkest hour of the old covenant holds still under the new, the existence of even a small remnant of believing Jews being itself the standing pledge that grace has not abandoned the seed of Abraham.
6:
"And if by grace, then is it no more of works: otherwise grace is no more grace. But if it be of works, then is it no more grace: otherwise work is no more work." The witnesses note the absolute antithesis of grace and works. On "if by grace, then no more of works," the grammatical voices note the exclusion. Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin note Paul presses the mutual exclusiveness: if salvation is by grace, it cannot be by works; the two are incompatible as grounds.

On "otherwise grace is no more grace," the witnesses note the logic. If works had any part, grace would cease to be grace (free, unearned); to mix works with grace destroys the nature of grace.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the principle: grace and works are absolutely opposed as grounds of election and salvation; the remnant is saved by grace alone, excluding all merit. The verse displays the absolute antithesis of grace and works.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul presses the mutual exclusiveness — if salvation is by grace, it cannot be by works, the two incompatible as grounds (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin); if works had any part, grace would cease to be grace (free, unearned), to mix works with grace destroying its nature. Calvin and Wesley note grace and works are absolutely opposed; the remnant saved by grace alone, excluding all merit. The verse displays the antithesis of grace and works. The verse marks the exclusion: grace not works. The verse displays the logic: grace would cease to be grace. The verse displays the incompatibility: the two opposed. The verse displays the ground: grace alone. The antithesis displays the purity of grace as the sole ground. The verse displays the mutual exclusion: grace or works. The verse displays the nature: grace must be free. The verse displays the principle: no merit. The absolute antithesis of grace and works — if by grace, then no more of works, otherwise grace is no more grace — displays the purity of grace as the sole ground of election and salvation: grace and works are mutually exclusive, for to admit works would destroy the very nature of grace as free and unearned. The verse displays the great principle that the remnant is saved by grace alone, and turns to the state of the rest of Israel (vv.7-10).
7:
"What then? Israel hath not obtained that which he seeketh for; but the election hath obtained it, and the rest were blinded." The witnesses note the election obtaining, the rest blinded. On "Israel hath not obtained," the grammatical voices note the failure. Barnes and Robertson note that Israel as a nation did not obtain the righteousness it sought (by works) — the mass failing.

On "the election hath obtained it," the witnesses note the remnant. The "election" (the elect remnant, those chosen by grace) did obtain it — the believing Jews receiving the righteousness of faith.

On "the rest were blinded," the eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the hardening. The rest (the unbelieving mass) were blinded (hardened, given over) — their own unbelief and God's judicial abandonment. The verse displays the election obtaining, the rest blinded.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Israel as a nation did not obtain the righteousness it sought by works (the mass failing, Barnes, Robertson), but "the election" (the elect remnant chosen by grace) did obtain it (the believing Jews receiving the righteousness of faith); and "the rest were blinded" (hardened, given over) through their own unbelief and God's judicial abandonment (Chrysostom, Calvin). The verse displays the election obtaining, the rest blinded. The verse marks the failure: Israel not obtaining. The verse displays the remnant: the election obtaining. The verse displays the hardening: the rest blinded. The verse displays the cause: unbelief and judgment. The election obtaining displays the division of Israel into the saved remnant and the hardened mass. The verse displays the failure: of the nation. The verse displays the success: of the elect. The verse displays the blinding: of the rest. The election obtaining and the rest blinded display the division of Israel: the elect remnant obtained the righteousness of faith, while the unbelieving mass was hardened, given over to blindness through their own unbelief and God's judgment. The verse displays the two outcomes within the nation, and grounds the blinding in the Scriptures (vv.8-10). The same gospel that proved life to the believing remnant became, by their own resistance, the occasion of a deeper hardening to the rest, so that the one preaching divided the nation into the saved and the judicially blinded.
8:
"(According as it is written, God hath given them the spirit of slumber, eyes that they should not see, and ears that they should not hear;) unto this day." The witnesses note the spirit of slumber given to the hardened. On the citation, the grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Isaiah 29:10 (and Deuteronomy 29:4) — God gave them a spirit of slumber (stupor, deep sleep), eyes that see not and ears that hear not.

On "God hath given them," the witnesses note the judicial act. Barnes and Calvin note the hardening is described as God's giving — a judicial act of withdrawing light and giving over to stupor, in punishment of their unbelief; not creating sin but abandoning to it.

On "unto this day," the eastern Theodoret notes the continuance: the blindness persisting to Paul's own time, the long-standing judicial stupor of unbelieving Israel. The verse displays the spirit of slumber given to the hardened.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Isaiah 29:10 (and Deuteronomy 29:4) — God gave them a spirit of slumber (stupor), eyes that see not and ears that hear not (Barnes, Robertson); the hardening described as God's giving, a judicial act of withdrawing light and giving over to stupor in punishment of unbelief, not creating sin but abandoning to it (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret notes the blindness persisting to Paul's day. The verse displays the spirit of slumber. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah. The verse displays the giving: a spirit of slumber. The verse displays the judicial act: withdrawing light. The verse displays the continuance: unto this day. The spirit of slumber displays the judicial hardening of unbelieving Israel. The verse displays the stupor: eyes and ears closed. The verse displays the giving over: in judgment. The verse displays the persistence: to this day. The spirit of slumber given to the hardened — eyes that see not, ears that hear not — displays the judicial hardening of unbelieving Israel: God, in righteous judgment of their unbelief, gave them over to a deep stupor, withdrawing the light, a blindness persisting to Paul's own day. The verse displays the Scripture's witness to the judicial blinding of the unbelieving mass, and confirms it from David (vv.9-10).
9:
"And David saith, Let their table be made a snare, and a trap, and a stumblingblock, and a recompence unto them:" The witnesses note David's imprecation on the unbelieving. On the citation, the grammatical voices note the source. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes David (Psalm 69:22-23) — an imprecation that their table (their feasting, prosperity, or their very privileges and law) become a snare, trap, stumbling-block, and recompence.

On "their table," the witnesses note the meaning. The "table" may denote their carnal security, their feasts, or the very Scriptures and privileges in which they trusted — turned into the occasion of their fall through their abuse of them.

The Reformed Calvin notes the justice: the very blessings, abused, become a curse; their privileges, misused, the occasion of their stumbling — a just recompence. The verse displays David's imprecation on the unbelieving.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes David (Psalm 69:22-23) — an imprecation that their table (their feasting, prosperity, or their very privileges and law) become a snare, trap, stumbling-block, and recompence (Barnes, Robertson); the "table" denoting their carnal security or the very Scriptures and privileges in which they trusted, turned into the occasion of their fall through their abuse. Calvin notes the justice: the very blessings, abused, become a curse, a just recompence. The verse displays David's imprecation. The verse marks the citation: Psalm 69. The verse displays the imprecation: their table a snare. The verse displays the meaning: privileges turned to ruin. The verse displays the justice: a recompence. David's imprecation displays the judgment on the unbelieving's abused privileges. The verse displays the snare: their table. The verse displays the abuse: blessings misused. The verse displays the recompence: just retribution. David's imprecation on the unbelieving — let their table be made a snare and a recompence — displays the judgment by which the very privileges of Israel, abused through unbelief, became the occasion of their fall: their feasting and security, their Scriptures and law, turned into a snare and a just recompence. The verse displays the prophetic witness to the self-inflicted ruin of the unbelieving, and continues the imprecation (v.10).
10:
"Let their eyes be darkened, that they may not see, and bow down their back alway." The brief verse completes David's imprecation. The grammatical voices note the citation (Psalm 69:23). Barnes and Robertson note the continued imprecation: that their eyes be darkened (blinded, unable to see) and their back bowed down always (bent under a burden, in servitude or affliction).

On "eyes darkened," the witnesses note the blindness. The darkening of the eyes parallels the spirit of slumber (v.8) — the judicial blindness of the unbelieving.

On "bow down their back alway," the eastern Theodoret notes the bondage: the bowed back denoting perpetual servitude or affliction — the long subjection of unbelieving Israel under the burden of their unbelief and dispersion. The verse displays David's completed imprecation: darkened eyes and bowed backs.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the continued imprecation (Psalm 69:23) — that their eyes be darkened (blinded) and their back bowed down always (bent under a burden, in servitude or affliction, Barnes, Robertson); the darkening of the eyes paralleling the spirit of slumber (v.8), the judicial blindness of the unbelieving. Theodoret notes the bowed back denotes perpetual servitude — the long subjection of unbelieving Israel. The verse displays David's completed imprecation. The verse marks the citation: Psalm 69. The verse displays the blindness: eyes darkened. The verse displays the bondage: back bowed always. The verse displays the parallel: with the spirit of slumber. David's completed imprecation displays the judicial blindness and bondage of the unbelieving. The verse displays the darkening: of the eyes. The verse displays the burden: the bowed back. The verse displays the perpetuity: always. David's completed imprecation — let their eyes be darkened and their back bowed down always — displays the judicial blindness and bondage of the unbelieving: their eyes darkened in stupor, their backs bent under perpetual servitude and affliction, the long subjection of unbelieving Israel under the burden of their unbelief. The verse closes the scriptural witness to the hardening, and turns from the fall to its merciful purpose (vv.11-15). These solemn words of the psalmist, taken up by the apostle, set the seal of prophecy upon the judicial blindness of unbelieving Israel, that none might think their hardening either accidental or unforeseen.
11:
"I say then, Have they stumbled that they should fall? God forbid: but rather through their fall salvation is come unto the Gentiles, for to provoke them to jealousy." The witnesses note the fall not final, but the occasion of the Gentiles' salvation. On "have they stumbled that they should fall?" the grammatical voices note the question. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks whether Israel's stumbling was meant to be a final, irrecoverable fall — and denies it ("God forbid"); their stumbling is not unto utter ruin.

On "through their fall salvation is come to the Gentiles," the witnesses note the providence. Barnes and Henry note that Israel's fall (rejection of the gospel) became the occasion of the gospel going to the Gentiles (Acts 13:46) — God overruling their unbelief for the world's salvation.

On "to provoke them to jealousy," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the design: the Gentiles' salvation was meant to provoke Israel to jealousy (emulation), and so ultimately to their own recovery — the fall serving their eventual restoration. The verse displays the fall not final, the occasion of the Gentiles' salvation and Israel's provoking.

The witnesses agree: Paul asks whether Israel's stumbling was a final, irrecoverable fall and denies it ("God forbid," Barnes, Robertson); rather, their fall (rejection of the gospel) became the occasion of salvation coming to the Gentiles (Acts 13:46), God overruling their unbelief for the world's good (Barnes, Henry) — and this to provoke Israel to jealousy (emulation), and so ultimately to their recovery (Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the fall not final. The verse marks the question: stumbled to fall? The verse displays the denial: God forbid. The verse displays the providence: salvation to the Gentiles. The verse displays the design: to provoke Israel to jealousy. The fall not final displays the merciful purpose served by Israel's stumbling. The verse displays the overruling: unbelief turned to the Gentiles' good. The verse displays the jealousy: provoking Israel. The verse displays the aim: their recovery. The fall not final, but the occasion of the Gentiles' salvation, displays the merciful purpose God served by Israel's stumbling: their rejection of the gospel opened the way for the Gentiles, and this was designed to provoke Israel to jealousy and so to their eventual recovery. The verse displays that Israel's fall was neither total nor final but overruled for good, and magnifies its fruitful effect (v.12).
12:
"Now if the fall of them be the riches of the world, and the diminishing of them the riches of the Gentiles; how much more their fulness?" The witnesses note the argument from the lesser to the greater: Israel's fulness. On "the fall of them the riches of the world," the grammatical voices note the present benefit. Barnes and Robertson note that Israel's fall (and diminishing) had already enriched the world and the Gentiles — by opening the gospel to them.

On "how much more their fulness?" the witnesses note the a fortiori argument. Barnes and Ellicott note "fulness" (pleroma) means the full restoration or ingathering of Israel; if their fall brought such blessing, how much more will their fulness (restoration) bring — a greater good to follow.

The Reformed Calvin notes the hope: the future restoration of Israel will bring even greater riches to the world than their fall did — the argument pointing to a great future blessing. The verse displays the argument from the lesser to the greater: the riches of Israel's fulness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Israel's fall (and diminishing) had already enriched the world and the Gentiles by opening the gospel to them (Barnes, Robertson); how much more their "fulness" (pleroma, the full restoration or ingathering of Israel) — if their fall brought such blessing, their restoration will bring far greater good (Barnes, Ellicott). Calvin notes the future restoration of Israel will bring even greater riches than their fall did. The verse displays the argument from lesser to greater. The verse marks the present benefit: the fall the riches of the world. The verse displays the a fortiori: how much more their fulness. The verse displays the restoration: the fulness of Israel. The verse displays the hope: a greater blessing. The argument displays the greater good of Israel's future restoration. The verse displays the fall: enriching the world. The verse displays the fulness: a greater riches. The verse displays the expectation: future blessing. The argument from the lesser to the greater — if the fall of Israel be the riches of the world, how much more their fulness — displays the greater good their future restoration will bring: if their stumbling enriched the Gentiles by opening the gospel, their full ingathering will bring far greater blessing to the world. The verse displays the hope of Israel's restoration and its overflowing benefit, and Paul's reason for magnifying his Gentile ministry (vv.13-15).
13:
"For I speak to you Gentiles, inasmuch as I am the apostle of the Gentiles, I magnify mine office:" The witnesses note Paul magnifying his Gentile office. On "I speak to you Gentiles," the grammatical voices note the address. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns directly to the Gentile believers, as the apostle specially commissioned to the Gentiles (Acts 9:15).

On "I magnify mine office," the witnesses note the honour. Paul honours (magnifies, glorifies) his ministry to the Gentiles — not from pride but to give it weight, and (v.14) to use it for a further end: the provoking of his own people.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the purpose: Paul exalts his Gentile office partly that, through the Gentiles' salvation, he might provoke and save some of his own nation. The verse displays Paul magnifying his Gentile office.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns directly to the Gentile believers, as the apostle specially commissioned to the Gentiles (Acts 9:15, Barnes, Robertson), and magnifies (honours, glorifies) his ministry — not from pride but to give it weight, and (v.14) to use it for a further end, the provoking of his own people. Calvin and Theodoret note Paul exalts his office partly that, through the Gentiles' salvation, he might save some of his nation. The verse displays Paul magnifying his office. The verse marks the address: to the Gentiles. The verse displays the office: apostle of the Gentiles. The verse displays the magnifying: honouring his ministry. The verse displays the purpose: to provoke his people. Paul magnifying his office displays his use of the Gentile ministry for Israel's good. The verse displays the commission: to the Gentiles. The verse displays the honour: magnifying it. The verse displays the aim: provoking the Jews. Paul magnifying his Gentile office displays his use of his ministry for the good even of his own nation: as the apostle to the Gentiles, he honours his office, partly that through the salvation of the Gentiles he might provoke and save some of his fellow Jews. The verse displays the apostle's love for Israel woven into his Gentile mission, and states the hoped-for effect (v.14).
14:
"If by any means I may provoke to emulation them which are my flesh, and might save some of them." The brief verse notes Paul's hope to provoke and save some of his kinsmen. The grammatical voices note the aim. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's purpose: by his Gentile ministry, to provoke to emulation (jealousy) his own people ("my flesh," his kinsmen) and so save some of them.

On "save some of them," the witnesses note the realism and hope. Paul hopes not to save all, but "some" — a realistic hope, yet a real one; even some saved would be precious.

The eastern Theodoret notes the love: Paul's whole desire bent toward the salvation of his nation, using even his Gentile success as a means to that end. The verse displays Paul's hope to provoke and save some of his kinsmen.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's purpose is, by his Gentile ministry, to provoke to emulation (jealousy) his own people ("my flesh," his kinsmen) and so save some of them (Barnes, Calvin) — hoping not to save all but "some," a realistic yet real hope. Theodoret notes Paul's whole desire bent toward the salvation of his nation. The verse displays Paul's hope. The verse marks the aim: to provoke his flesh. The verse displays the means: emulation. The verse displays the hope: save some. The verse displays the love: for his nation. Paul's hope displays his love for Israel and his use of his ministry for their salvation. The verse displays the provoking: to jealousy. The verse displays the realism: some, not all. The verse displays the desire: their salvation. Paul's hope to provoke and save some of his kinsmen displays his deep love for Israel and his strategic use of his Gentile ministry for their good: by stirring his own people to jealousy at the Gentiles' salvation, he hoped to save at least some of them. The verse displays the apostle's tender, realistic hope for his nation, and returns to the great theme of their restoration (v.15). In this the apostle shows that his labours among the nations were never a forsaking of his own people, but the very instrument by which he hoped, under God, to win back some portion of the kindred he so dearly loved.
15:
"For if the casting away of them be the reconciling of the world, what shall the receiving of them be, but life from the dead?" The witnesses note Israel's reception as life from the dead. On "the casting away... the reconciling of the world," the grammatical voices note the present effect. Barnes and Robertson note that Israel's rejection (casting away) had occasioned the reconciling of the Gentile world — the gospel going to the nations.

On "the receiving of them... life from the dead," the great image divides interpreters. Barnes, Calvin, and Wesley take "life from the dead" figuratively: the future restoration of Israel will bring a great spiritual revival, a resurrection of religion in the Gentile world — overflowing life. The eastern Chrysostom, Theodoret, and Ellicott take it more literally: Israel's conversion will be the signal for the general resurrection at the end.

The witnesses note the a fortiori force: if Israel's fall brought such good, their reception will bring far greater — a glorious revival, or the consummation itself. The verse displays Israel's reception as life from the dead.

The witnesses agree: Israel's casting away occasioned the reconciling of the Gentile world (the gospel to the nations, Barnes, Robertson) — so how much more their reception, which shall be "life from the dead." But the image divides them: Barnes, Calvin, and Wesley take it figuratively (a great spiritual revival, a resurrection of religion in the world, overflowing life); Chrysostom, Theodoret, and Ellicott take it more literally (Israel's conversion the signal for the general resurrection at the end). The a fortiori force is plain: if the fall brought such good, the reception brings far greater. The verse displays Israel's reception as life from the dead. The verse marks the present effect: the casting away reconciling the world. The verse displays the greater: the receiving. The verse displays the debate: figurative revival or literal resurrection. The verse displays the force: how much more. Israel's reception displays the surpassing blessing of their future restoration. The verse displays the reconciliation: the world. The verse displays the life: from the dead. The verse displays the hope: a glorious revival or the end. Israel's reception as life from the dead displays the surpassing blessing of their future restoration: if their casting away reconciled the Gentile world, their reception will bring "life from the dead" — whether (figuratively) a glorious spiritual revival throughout the world, or (literally) the signal of the general resurrection at the end. The verse displays the immense good bound up with Israel's restoration, and grounds it in the holiness of the root (v.16).
16:
"For if the firstfruit be holy, the lump is also holy: and if the root be holy, so are the branches." The witnesses note the holy root and the branches. On "the firstfruit holy, the lump holy," the grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note the image from Numbers 15:19-21 — the firstfruit (the first dough offered) consecrating the whole lump; so the consecration of the part extends to the whole.

On "the root holy, the branches holy," the witnesses note the second figure. Barnes, Calvin, and Robertson note the "firstfruit" and "root" are the patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob); their consecration (in the covenant) extends to their descendants — so Israel is, in a sense, set apart, the branches partaking of the root's holiness.

The Reformed Calvin notes the holiness is external and relative (covenantal consecration, not personal salvation) — the nation set apart in the fathers, the privileges transmitted; a ground of hope for Israel's restoration. The verse displays the holy root and branches, the ground of hope for Israel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the image from Numbers 15 — the firstfruit (first dough offered) consecrating the whole lump, and the root consecrating the branches (Barnes, Robertson); the "firstfruit" and "root" being the patriarchs, whose consecration in the covenant extends to their descendants, so Israel is set apart, the branches partaking of the root's holiness (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the holiness is external and relative (covenantal consecration, not personal salvation), the privileges transmitted, a ground of hope for Israel's restoration. The verse displays the holy root and branches. The verse marks the figures: firstfruit and root. The verse displays the consecration: the part hallowing the whole. The verse displays the patriarchs: the root. The verse displays the holiness: external and covenantal. The holy root displays the ground of hope for Israel's restoration. The verse displays the firstfruit: the patriarchs. The verse displays the branches: their descendants. The verse displays the hope: Israel set apart. The holy root and the branches — if the firstfruit be holy, so is the lump — display the ground of hope for Israel's restoration: the patriarchs, the firstfruit and root, were consecrated in the covenant, and their descendants partake of that external, relative holiness. The verse displays the covenantal consecration of Israel in the fathers, a pledge that the nation will yet be restored, and develops the olive-tree image (vv.17-24).
17:
"And if some of the branches be broken off, and thou, being a wild olive tree, wert graffed in among them, and with them partakest of the root and fatness of the olive tree;" The witnesses note the olive tree: Gentiles grafted in. On "some of the branches broken off," the grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note the image of the olive tree: some natural branches (unbelieving Jews) were broken off for unbelief.

On "thou, being a wild olive, wert graffed in," the witnesses note the grafting. Barnes and Robertson note the Gentiles (a wild olive, naturally unfruitful) were grafted in among the natural branches — and Robertson notes Ramsay's point that grafting a wild olive onto an old tree could reinvigorate it, though Paul notes (v.24) this was "contrary to nature."

On "partakest of the root and fatness," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the meaning: the Gentiles partake of the root (the patriarchs, the covenant) and its fatness (the rich blessings and promises) — their salvation drawn from the Jewish stock. The verse displays the Gentiles grafted into the olive tree.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the olive-tree image — some natural branches (unbelieving Jews) broken off for unbelief, and the Gentiles (a wild olive, naturally unfruitful) grafted in among them (Barnes, Robertson, who notes Ramsay's point about reinvigorating the old tree, though "contrary to nature," v.24) — partaking of the root (the patriarchs, the covenant) and its fatness (the rich blessings and promises, Calvin, Theodoret), their salvation drawn from the Jewish stock. The verse displays the Gentiles grafted in. The verse marks the breaking: some branches off. The verse displays the grafting: the wild olive in. The verse displays the partaking: of root and fatness. The verse displays the source: the Jewish stock. The olive tree displays the Gentiles' incorporation into the covenant people. The verse displays the breaking: unbelieving Jews. The verse displays the grafting: believing Gentiles. The verse displays the nourishment: from the root. The Gentiles grafted into the olive tree, partaking of the root and fatness, display their incorporation into the covenant people: some unbelieving Jews were broken off, and the Gentiles, a wild olive, were grafted in to share the rich blessings flowing from the patriarchal root. The verse displays that the Gentiles' salvation is drawn from the Jewish stock, and warns them against pride (vv.18-22).
18:
"Boast not against the branches. But if thou boast, thou bearest not the root, but the root thee." The witnesses note the warning against Gentile boasting. On "boast not against the branches," the grammatical voices note the caution. Barnes and Robertson note Paul warns the Gentiles not to boast against (glory over) the broken-off branches (the rejected Jews) — the danger of pride in their privileged position.

On "thou bearest not the root, but the root thee," the witnesses note the dependence. Barnes and Calvin note the corrective: the Gentiles do not support the root but are supported by it; their blessings are derived, not original — drawn from the patriarchal stock and covenant.

The eastern Theodoret notes the humility: the Gentiles need the root, not the root them; all their salvation flows from the holy root, so they have no ground for boasting. The verse displays the warning against Gentile boasting.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul warns the Gentiles not to boast against (glory over) the broken-off branches, the rejected Jews (Barnes, Robertson) — the danger of pride in their privileged position — for they do not support the root but are supported by it; their blessings derived, not original, drawn from the patriarchal stock and covenant (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret notes the Gentiles need the root, not the root them. The verse displays the warning against boasting. The verse marks the caution: boast not. The verse displays the dependence: borne by the root. The verse displays the derivation: blessings not original. The verse displays the humility: no ground for pride. The warning displays the Gentiles' dependence on the covenant root. The verse displays the prohibition: against boasting. The verse displays the support: by the root. The verse displays the source: the patriarchal stock. The warning against Gentile boasting — thou bearest not the root, but the root thee — displays the Gentiles' dependence on the covenant root: their salvation is derived from the patriarchal stock, not original to themselves, so they have no ground to boast against the broken-off branches. The verse displays the humility that befits the grafted-in Gentiles, and meets their imagined self-justification (vv.19-21).
19:
"Thou wilt say then, The branches were broken off, that I might be graffed in." The brief verse notes the Gentile's self-justifying objection. The grammatical voices note the cavil. Barnes and Robertson note Paul anticipates the Gentile's argument: the branches (Jews) were broken off precisely so that I (the Gentile) might be grafted in — a ground, the objector imagines, for self-congratulation.

On the objection, the witnesses note the implied pride. The Gentile presumes a kind of preference: their rejection made room for me, so I have cause to glory.

The Reformed Calvin notes the irony: even if true, this should humble rather than exalt — for the breaking off was through unbelief and the grafting through faith, both calling for fear, not pride. The verse displays the Gentile's self-justifying objection.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul anticipates the Gentile's argument — the branches were broken off precisely so that I might be grafted in (Barnes, Robertson) — a ground the objector imagines for self-congratulation, presuming a preference: their rejection made room for me. Calvin notes the irony: even if true, this should humble rather than exalt, for the breaking off was through unbelief and the grafting through faith, both calling for fear. The verse displays the self-justifying objection. The verse marks the cavil: broken off for me. The verse displays the implied pride: cause to glory. The verse displays the presumption: a preference. The verse displays the irony: should humble. The objection displays the Gentile's tendency to self-congratulation. The verse displays the argument: branches broken for me. The verse displays the pride: imagined preference. The verse displays the correction to come: fear, not pride. The Gentile's self-justifying objection — the branches were broken off that I might be grafted in — displays the tendency to self-congratulation: the Gentile presumes that the Jews' rejection was a preference shown to him. The verse displays the proud reasoning Paul will correct, showing that the true ground was unbelief and faith, calling not for boasting but for humble fear (vv.20-21). By framing the objection in the mouth of the imagined Gentile, the apostle exposes the secret pride that lurks in the heart even of the truly grafted, that he might pluck it up by the roots before it could grow.
20:
"Well; because of unbelief they were broken off, and thou standest by faith. Be not highminded, but fear:" The witnesses note the true ground — unbelief and faith — and the call to fear. On "because of unbelief they were broken off," the grammatical voices note the cause. Barnes and Robertson note Paul corrects the Gentile: the Jews were broken off not to make room for the Gentiles as a preference, but because of their unbelief — the true cause was their own sin.

On "thou standest by faith," the witnesses note the condition. Barnes and Wesley note the Gentile stands (in his privileged place) by faith — not by merit or natural right, but by faith; and this conditionally, as the Jews fell by unbelief.

On "be not highminded, but fear," the witnesses note the exhortation. Barnes and Calvin note "fear" is not terror but humble watchfulness, the opposite of pride — the Gentile is to be humble and circumspect, lest he too fall as the Jews did. The verse displays the true ground and the call to humble fear.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul corrects the Gentile — the Jews were broken off because of their unbelief (the true cause their own sin, not a preference, Barnes, Robertson) — and the Gentile stands by faith (not by merit or natural right, and conditionally, Barnes, Wesley); therefore "be not highminded, but fear" — not terror but humble watchfulness, the opposite of pride (Barnes, Calvin). The verse displays the true ground and the call to fear. The verse marks the cause: unbelief. The verse displays the condition: standing by faith. The verse displays the exhortation: be not highminded, but fear. The verse displays the humility: watchful, not proud. The true ground displays the humble fear that befits the believer. The verse displays the breaking off: through unbelief. The verse displays the standing: by faith. The verse displays the warning: against pride. The true ground — unbelief and faith — and the call to fear display the humility that befits the Gentile believer: the Jews fell by unbelief and the Gentile stands by faith, not by merit, so he must not be high-minded but watchful and humble, lest he too fall. The verse displays the conditional, faith-dependent standing of the believer and the call to godly fear, reinforced by the warning of v.21.
21:
"For if God spared not the natural branches, take heed lest he also spare not thee." The witnesses note the warning from the Jews' rejection. On "if God spared not the natural branches," the grammatical voices note the precedent. Barnes and Robertson note the argument: if God did not spare the natural branches (the Jews, his ancient people) when they fell into unbelief, he will not spare the grafted-in Gentiles either if they likewise fall.

On "take heed lest he spare not thee," the witnesses note the warning. The Gentile has no security greater than the Jew had; the same principle that rejected the unbelieving Jews would reject unbelieving Gentiles.

The schools read it by their view. The Reformed Calvin holds the warning addresses the whole body of professing Gentiles (the elect persevere, but the warning subdues the flesh); the wesleyan-arminian Wesley holds it a real warning that the Gentile, like the Jew, may fall and be cut off. The verse displays the warning from the Jews' rejection.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: if God did not spare the natural branches (the Jews, his ancient people) when they fell into unbelief, he will not spare the grafted-in Gentiles if they likewise fall (Barnes, Robertson) — the Gentile having no security greater than the Jew had. The schools read it variously: Calvin holds the warning addresses the whole body of professing Gentiles (the elect persevere, but the warning subdues the flesh); Wesley holds it a real warning that the Gentile may fall and be cut off. The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the precedent: God spared not the natural branches. The verse displays the warning: lest he spare not thee. The verse displays the principle: no greater security. The verse displays the reading: the body vs the individual. The warning displays the seriousness of the Gentile's position. The verse displays the precedent: the Jews not spared. The verse displays the danger: the Gentile too. The verse displays the humbling: no security in self. The warning from the Jews' rejection — if God spared not the natural branches, take heed lest he spare not thee — displays the seriousness of the Gentile's position: having no security greater than the Jew had, the Gentile is warned that the same principle which rejected the unbelieving Jews would reject unbelieving Gentiles. The verse displays the humbling caution against presumption, read by the Reformed of the professing body and by the Arminian as a real personal warning, and unfolds the goodness and severity of God (v.22).
22:
"Behold therefore the goodness and severity of God: on them which fell, severity; but toward thee, goodness, if thou continue in his goodness: otherwise thou also shalt be cut off." The witnesses note the goodness and severity of God — and the perseverance crux. On "the goodness and severity of God," the grammatical voices note the two-fold display. Barnes and Robertson note Paul bids the Gentile behold both: severity toward the fallen Jews (the cutting off), and goodness toward the believing Gentiles (their admission) — both to be contemplated for profit.

On "if thou continue in his goodness, otherwise cut off," the great debate divides. The grammatical-historical voices (Barnes, Robertson) and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley take it as a real condition: the continuance of the Gentile's standing depends on his persevering in faith; obedience an indispensable condition, otherwise he too shall be cut off. The Reformed Calvin holds the warning addresses the body of professing Gentiles, not the elect individually (whose perseverance flows from election); the threat subdues the flesh, but the truly elect cannot finally be cut off.

The witnesses note the purpose: to keep the Gentile humble and watchful, casting him on God's grace. The verse displays the goodness and severity of God and the conditional warning.

The witnesses agree Paul bids the Gentile behold both the goodness and severity of God — severity toward the fallen Jews, goodness toward the believing Gentiles (Barnes, Robertson) — but divide on "if thou continue." The grammatical-historical voices and Wesley take it as a real condition: the Gentile's standing depends on persevering in faith, obedience an indispensable condition, otherwise cut off. Calvin holds the warning addresses the professing body, not the elect individually (whose perseverance flows from election), the threat subduing the flesh while the truly elect cannot finally fall. The verse displays the goodness and severity. The verse marks the two-fold display: severity and goodness. The verse displays the condition: if thou continue. The verse displays the debate: real apostasy or the professing body. The verse displays the purpose: humility and watchfulness. The goodness and severity display the call to persevere in God's goodness. The verse displays the severity: on the fallen. The verse displays the goodness: toward believers. The verse displays the crux: conditional security. The goodness and severity of God — severity on the fallen, goodness toward thee, if thou continue — display the call to persevere in God's grace: the Gentile must continue in faith or be cut off as the Jews were. The verse opens the great perseverance debate: the grammatical-historical and Arminian reading it as a real condition on the individual believer's continuance, the Reformed as a warning to the professing body that subdues the flesh while the elect, kept by God, persevere. The verse displays the solemn call to humble continuance, and holds out hope for the restoration of the fallen (vv.23-24).
23:
"And they also, if they abide not still in unbelief, shall be graffed in: for God is able to graff them in again." The witnesses note the hope of Israel's regrafting. On "if they abide not still in unbelief," the grammatical voices note the condition. Barnes and Robertson note the door is open for the Jews: if they do not continue in unbelief (if they believe), they shall be grafted in again — their restoration conditioned on faith.

On "God is able to graff them in again," the witnesses note the divine power. Barnes and Calvin note God's power to restore the fallen Jews — their regrafting is not beyond him; he who broke them off can graft them in again upon their believing.

The eastern Theodoret notes the hope held out: the offer remains open to the unbelieving Jews; their unbelief, not any decree, bars them, and faith would restore them. The verse displays the hope of Israel's regrafting.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the door is open for the Jews — if they do not continue in unbelief (if they believe), they shall be grafted in again, their restoration conditioned on faith (Barnes, Robertson); and God is able to graft them in again, his power to restore the fallen not exhausted (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret notes the offer remains open, their unbelief alone barring them. The verse displays the hope of Israel's regrafting. The verse marks the condition: if they abide not in unbelief. The verse displays the restoration: grafted in again. The verse displays the power: God is able. The verse displays the hope: the offer open. The hope of regrafting displays the open door for Israel's restoration. The verse displays the condition: faith. The verse displays the power: God able. The verse displays the offer: still open. The hope of Israel's regrafting — if they abide not in unbelief, they shall be grafted in again — displays the open door for their restoration: their unbelief alone bars them, and upon believing they would be restored, for God is able to graft them in again. The verse displays the gracious possibility of Israel's recovery, conditioned on faith and grounded in God's power, and presses the argument from nature (v.24).
24:
"For if thou wert cut out of the olive tree which is wild by nature, and wert graffed contrary to nature into a good olive tree: how much more shall these, which be the natural branches, be graffed into their own olive tree?" The witnesses note the argument from nature: how much more the natural branches. On "cut out of the wild olive... graffed contrary to nature," the grammatical voices note the unusual grafting. Barnes and Robertson note the Gentiles were grafted "contrary to nature" — a wild olive grafted into a good tree, the reverse of the usual horticultural practice.

On "how much more the natural branches," the witnesses note the a fortiori argument. Barnes and Wesley note that if the Gentiles, against nature, could be grafted in, how much more readily can the natural branches (the Jews) be grafted back into their own tree — their restoration being more "natural" than the Gentiles' ingrafting.

The eastern Theodoret notes the encouragement: the regrafting of the Jews, being according to nature, is the easier and more to be expected — a strong ground of hope for Israel. The verse displays the argument from nature: the easier regrafting of the natural branches.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Gentiles were grafted "contrary to nature" — a wild olive into a good tree, reversing the usual practice (Barnes, Robertson) — so, by an a fortiori argument, if they against nature could be grafted in, how much more readily can the natural branches (the Jews) be grafted back into their own tree, their restoration being more "natural" (Barnes, Wesley). Theodoret notes the regrafting of the Jews, being according to nature, is the easier and more expected, a strong ground of hope. The verse displays the argument from nature. The verse marks the unusual: grafted contrary to nature. The verse displays the a fortiori: how much more the natural branches. The verse displays the ease: the natural regrafting. The verse displays the hope: Israel's restoration. The argument from nature displays the ready hope of Israel's restoration. The verse displays the wild olive: against nature. The verse displays the natural branches: their own tree. The verse displays the expectation: the easier regrafting. The argument from nature — if the wild olive was grafted contrary to nature, how much more the natural branches into their own tree — displays the ready hope of Israel's restoration: their regrafting, being according to nature, is even more to be expected than the Gentiles' ingrafting against nature. The verse displays the strong ground of hope for Israel's recovery, and unfolds the mystery of their future restoration (vv.25-27).
25:
"For I would not, brethren, that ye should be ignorant of this mystery, lest ye should be wise in your own conceits; that blindness in part is happened to Israel, until the fulness of the Gentiles be come in." The witnesses note the mystery: Israel's partial blindness until the Gentiles' fulness. On "this mystery," the grammatical voices note the revelation. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note "mystery" means a truth once hidden, now revealed by God — here, the divine purpose for Jew and Gentile, especially Israel's present exclusion and future restoration.

On "blindness in part... until the fulness of the Gentiles," the witnesses note the limited and temporary hardening. Barnes and Calvin note "in part" qualifies Israel — the blindness extends to some, not all (a remnant believing) — and is temporary, "until the fulness of the Gentiles be come in" (the full ingathering of the Gentile nations).

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and eastern Theodoret note the lesson: Israel is neither totally nor finally rejected; the Gentile must not be conceited, for the hardening will end when the Gentile harvest is complete. The verse displays the mystery: Israel's partial blindness until the Gentiles' fulness.

The witnesses agree: Paul reveals a "mystery" (a truth once hidden, now made known, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson) lest the Gentiles be conceited — that blindness "in part" (extending to some, not all, a remnant believing, Barnes, Calvin) has happened to Israel "until the fulness of the Gentiles be come in" (the full ingathering of the Gentile nations). The hardening is thus limited and temporary. Wesley and Theodoret note Israel is neither totally nor finally rejected, the hardening ending when the Gentile harvest is complete. The verse displays the mystery. The verse marks the revelation: the mystery made known. The verse displays the limitation: blindness in part. The verse displays the duration: until the Gentiles' fulness. The verse displays the lesson: no conceit. The mystery displays the temporary, partial nature of Israel's hardening. The verse displays the disclosure: the divine purpose. The verse displays the remnant: not all blinded. The verse displays the term: the Gentiles' fulness. The mystery — blindness in part has happened to Israel until the fulness of the Gentiles be come in — displays the temporary and partial nature of Israel's hardening: it extends to some, not all, and endures only until the full number of the Gentiles is gathered. The verse displays the divine purpose that keeps the Gentile humble and holds out hope for Israel, and announces the climactic promise (v.26).
26:
"And so all Israel shall be saved: as it is written, There shall come out of Sion the Deliverer, and shall turn away ungodliness from Jacob:" The witnesses note the climactic promise: all Israel shall be saved — the central crux. On "all Israel shall be saved," the great debate divides. The grammatical-historical and most Reformed voices (Gill, Barnes, JFB, Robertson leaning, Wesley, Theodoret) take "all Israel" as the Jewish nation as a whole — a future national conversion of the Jews (not every individual, but the body of the nation) when the fulness of the Gentiles is come in. Reformed Calvin, however, takes "all Israel" as the whole spiritual Israel — Jews and Gentiles together, the entire people of God (the Israel of God, Galatians 6:16).

On "the Deliverer from Sion," the witnesses note the citation (Isaiah 59:20). The Deliverer (the Messiah, Christ) comes from/to Zion to turn away ungodliness from Jacob — accomplishing the salvation of Israel; Paul applies it to Christ.

The witnesses note the hope: whether a national conversion of the Jews or the completion of the whole elect, the promise points to a glorious consummation of God's saving purpose. The verse displays the climactic promise and its great interpretive crux.

The witnesses agree the verse is the climax — "all Israel shall be saved" — proved by Isaiah 59:20 (the Deliverer from Zion turning away ungodliness from Jacob, applied to Christ) — but divide sharply on "all Israel." The grammatical-historical and most Reformed (Gill, Barnes, JFB, Robertson leaning, Wesley, Theodoret) take it as the Jewish nation as a whole — a future national conversion (the body, not every individual). Calvin takes it as the whole spiritual Israel — Jews and Gentiles together, the entire people of God (Galatians 6:16). The verse displays the climactic promise. The verse marks the promise: all Israel saved. The verse displays the crux: the nation or the whole elect. The verse displays the proof: the Deliverer from Zion. The verse displays the hope: a glorious consummation. The promise displays the saving purpose of God for Israel. The verse displays the salvation: of all Israel. The verse displays the debate: national or spiritual. The verse displays the Deliverer: Christ. The climactic promise — all Israel shall be saved — and its proof from the Deliverer who comes from Zion display the consummation of God's saving purpose, and open the chapter's central crux: whether "all Israel" means the Jewish nation as a whole in a future national conversion (the majority view) or the entire spiritual Israel of Jew and Gentile together (Calvin). The verse displays the glorious hope of Israel's salvation, however interpreted, and grounds it in God's covenant (v.27).
27:
"For this is my covenant unto them, when I shall take away their sins." The brief verse notes the covenant of forgiveness. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul continues the quotation (Isaiah 59:21; 27:9) — this is God's covenant with Israel: to take away their sins.

On "when I shall take away their sins," the witnesses note the substance. The covenant's blessing is the forgiveness of sins — the removal of Israel's iniquity, the heart of the new covenant fulfilled in Christ.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the ground: Israel's restoration rests on God's covenant of grace, which secures the taking away of their sins — their salvation grounded in the divine promise. The verse displays the covenant of forgiveness for Israel.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul continues the quotation (Isaiah 59:21; 27:9) — this is God's covenant with Israel, to take away their sins (Barnes, Robertson); the covenant's blessing the forgiveness of sins, the removal of Israel's iniquity, the heart of the new covenant fulfilled in Christ. Calvin and Theodoret note Israel's restoration rests on God's covenant of grace, securing the taking away of their sins. The verse displays the covenant of forgiveness. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah. The verse displays the covenant: God's promise to Israel. The verse displays the blessing: sins taken away. The verse displays the ground: the covenant of grace. The covenant displays the forgiveness secured for Israel. The verse displays the promise: the covenant. The verse displays the substance: forgiveness. The verse displays the ground: grace. The covenant of forgiveness for Israel — this is my covenant, when I shall take away their sins — displays the gracious ground of their restoration: God's covenant secures the removal of Israel's sins, the heart of the new covenant fulfilled in Christ. The verse displays that Israel's future salvation rests on the divine covenant of grace, and turns to the abiding love of God for the nation (vv.28-29). The covenant thus cited binds the future restoration of Israel to the same gracious foundation on which the whole gospel rests, the free forgiveness of sins secured not by the law but by the blood of the Deliverer.
28:
"As concerning the gospel, they are enemies for your sakes: but as touching the election, they are beloved for the fathers' sakes." The witnesses note Israel as enemies yet beloved. On "as concerning the gospel, they are enemies for your sakes," the grammatical voices note the present state. Barnes and Robertson note that, in respect to the gospel, the unbelieving Jews are enemies (treated as such, rejected) — and this "for your sakes," i.e., their rejection opened the gospel to the Gentiles.

On "as touching the election, beloved for the fathers' sakes," the witnesses note the abiding love. Barnes and Calvin note that in respect to God's election (his choice of the nation), they remain beloved — for the sake of the fathers (the patriarchs), to whom the promises were made; God's love to the nation enduring because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

The eastern Theodoret notes the two-fold relation: enemies as to the gospel now, yet beloved as to the election — the ground of hope for their restoration. The verse displays Israel as enemies yet beloved.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: in respect to the gospel, the unbelieving Jews are enemies (rejected) "for your sakes" — their rejection opening the gospel to the Gentiles (Barnes, Robertson); but in respect to God's election, they remain beloved "for the fathers' sakes" — God's love to the nation enduring because of his covenant with the patriarchs (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret notes the two-fold relation: enemies as to the gospel, yet beloved as to the election, the ground of hope. The verse displays Israel as enemies yet beloved. The verse marks the present state: enemies as to the gospel. The verse displays the cause: for your sakes. The verse displays the abiding love: beloved for the fathers. The verse displays the ground: God's covenant. Israel enemies yet beloved displays the two-fold relation grounding their hope. The verse displays the enmity: as to the gospel. The verse displays the love: as to the election. The verse displays the fathers: the covenant ground. Israel as enemies yet beloved — enemies as to the gospel for your sakes, beloved as to the election for the fathers' sakes — displays the two-fold relation that grounds their hope: though now rejected for unbelief (to the Gentiles' benefit), they remain the objects of God's enduring love for the sake of the patriarchs and his covenant. The verse displays that God's love to Israel has not ceased, and confirms it by the irrevocability of his gifts (v.29).
29:
"For the gifts and calling of God are without repentance." The brief verse notes the irrevocability of God's gifts and calling. The grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note "without repentance" means irrevocable, not to be repented of or recalled — God does not change his mind about the gifts (privileges, promises) and calling he bestowed on Israel.

On the application, the witnesses note the security. Barnes and Calvin note this grounds the hope of Israel's restoration: since God's gifts and calling to the nation are irrevocable, his purpose toward them stands, and they shall be restored.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the broader truth: God's calling is unchangeable; what he has freely given he does not withdraw — a comfort applicable to all the called. The verse displays the irrevocability of God's gifts and calling.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "without repentance" means irrevocable — God does not change his mind about the gifts (privileges, promises) and calling he bestowed on Israel (Barnes, Robertson); this grounds the hope of their restoration, since God's purpose toward them stands (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Theodoret note the broader truth: God's calling is unchangeable, a comfort to all the called. The verse displays the irrevocability of God's gifts and calling. The verse marks the principle: without repentance. The verse displays the meaning: irrevocable. The verse displays the application: Israel's restoration secured. The verse displays the broader truth: the calling unchangeable. The irrevocability displays the security of God's purpose toward Israel. The verse displays the gifts: not recalled. The verse displays the calling: unchangeable. The verse displays the hope: restoration sure. The irrevocability of God's gifts and calling — they are without repentance — displays the security of his purpose toward Israel: since what God has freely given and called he does not withdraw or repent of, the nation's restoration is sure, grounded in his unchangeable faithfulness. The verse displays the great comfort of God's irrevocable calling, applicable to all the called, and unfolds the mutual mercy shown to Jew and Gentile (vv.30-32).
30:
"For as ye in times past have not believed God, yet have now obtained mercy through their unbelief:" The brief verse notes the Gentiles' mercy through Israel's unbelief. The grammatical voices note the parallel. Barnes and Robertson note Paul reminds the Gentiles that they once did not believe God (were disobedient, in heathen darkness) yet have now obtained mercy — and this "through their (Israel's) unbelief," for Israel's rejection opened the gospel to them.

On the providence, the witnesses note the interweaving. The Gentiles' mercy came by occasion of the Jews' unbelief — God overruling the one for the other.

The eastern Theodoret notes the humbling lesson: the Gentiles, once disobedient, received mercy through Israel's fall — a reason for humility and gratitude, not pride. The verse displays the Gentiles' mercy obtained through Israel's unbelief.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul reminds the Gentiles that they once did not believe God (were disobedient, in heathen darkness) yet have now obtained mercy "through their (Israel's) unbelief" — Israel's rejection opening the gospel to them (Barnes, Robertson); the Gentiles' mercy coming by occasion of the Jews' unbelief, God overruling the one for the other. Theodoret notes the humbling lesson: the Gentiles, once disobedient, received mercy through Israel's fall, a reason for humility. The verse displays the Gentiles' mercy through Israel's unbelief. The verse marks the past: ye believed not. The verse displays the present: obtained mercy. The verse displays the occasion: through their unbelief. The verse displays the lesson: humility. The Gentiles' mercy displays the providence interweaving Jew and Gentile. The verse displays the former disobedience: of the Gentiles. The verse displays the mercy: now obtained. The verse displays the occasion: Israel's unbelief. The Gentiles' mercy obtained through Israel's unbelief displays the providence interweaving the destinies of Jew and Gentile: the Gentiles, once disobedient, received mercy by occasion of Israel's fall, which opened the gospel to them. The verse displays the humbling truth that the Gentiles owe their mercy partly to the Jews' rejection, and completes the parallel (v.31). The apostle thus turns the Gentiles own history into an argument for humility, reminding them that their present standing in mercy was itself born out of a season of disobedience no less real than that in which Israel now lies.
31:
"Even so have these also now not believed, that through your mercy they also may obtain mercy." The witnesses note Israel's hoped-for mercy through the Gentiles' mercy. On "these also now not believed," the grammatical voices note the present unbelief. Barnes and Robertson note that, just as the Gentiles were once disobedient, so now the Jews are in unbelief — the parallel reversed.

On "that through your mercy they also may obtain mercy," the witnesses note the design. Barnes and Ellicott note the purpose: that through the mercy shown to the Gentiles, the Jews also may (be provoked to) obtain mercy — the Gentiles' salvation becoming, in God's plan, a means to Israel's restoration.

The eastern Theodoret notes the beautiful reciprocity: as the Jews' fall brought the Gentiles mercy, so the Gentiles' mercy is to bring the Jews mercy — a mutual interweaving toward universal grace. The verse displays Israel's hoped-for mercy through the Gentiles' mercy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: just as the Gentiles were once disobedient, so now the Jews are in unbelief (the parallel reversed, Barnes, Robertson) — that through the mercy shown to the Gentiles, the Jews also may (be provoked to) obtain mercy, the Gentiles' salvation becoming a means to Israel's restoration (Barnes, Ellicott). Theodoret notes the reciprocity: as the Jews' fall brought the Gentiles mercy, so the Gentiles' mercy is to bring the Jews mercy. The verse displays Israel's hoped-for mercy. The verse marks the unbelief: the Jews now. The verse displays the design: through your mercy. The verse displays the hope: they may obtain mercy. The verse displays the reciprocity: mutual interweaving. Israel's hoped-for mercy displays the reciprocal design of grace. The verse displays the present: Jewish unbelief. The verse displays the means: the Gentiles' mercy. The verse displays the end: Israel's mercy. Israel's hoped-for mercy through the Gentiles' mercy displays the reciprocal design of grace: as the Jews' fall brought the Gentiles mercy, so now the mercy shown to the Gentiles is to provoke and bring the Jews to mercy. The verse displays the beautiful interweaving of Jew and Gentile in God's saving plan, and reaches its sublime conclusion (v.32).
32:
"For God hath concluded them all in unbelief, that he might have mercy upon all." The witnesses note God shutting up all in unbelief that he might have mercy on all. On "concluded them all in unbelief," the grammatical voices note the shutting up. Barnes and Robertson note "concluded" (sunekleisen) means shut up together, confined — God has shut up all (Jew and Gentile) under unbelief, as in a prison, that none might claim salvation by their own merit.

On "that he might have mercy upon all," the witnesses note the design. Barnes and Calvin note the purpose: that he might have mercy on all — both Jew and Gentile; God shut up all under sin precisely so that all might be saved by mercy alone, not works.

On the scope of "all," the witnesses note the meaning. Most take "all" as both classes (Jews and Gentiles), not every individual — mercy offered to all kinds, the whole shut up that grace might reign. The Reformed Calvin notes God's design to magnify his mercy by leaving none with a ground of boasting. The verse displays God shutting up all in unbelief that he might have mercy on all.

The witnesses agree: "concluded" (sunekleisen) means shut up together, confined — God has shut up all (Jew and Gentile) under unbelief, as in a prison, that none might claim salvation by merit (Barnes, Robertson) — that he might have mercy upon all, both classes; God shutting up all under sin precisely so that all might be saved by mercy alone (Barnes, Calvin). Most take "all" as both classes (not every individual), mercy offered to all kinds. Calvin notes God's design to magnify his mercy by leaving none a ground of boasting. The verse displays God shutting up all in unbelief. The verse marks the shutting up: all in unbelief. The verse displays the design: mercy upon all. The verse displays the scope: Jew and Gentile. The verse displays the purpose: grace magnified. God shutting up all displays the design to save all by mercy alone. The verse displays the confinement: under unbelief. The verse displays the mercy: upon all. The verse displays the exclusion: of all boasting. God shutting up all in unbelief, that he might have mercy upon all, displays the grand design of his dealing with Jew and Gentile: he has confined all under unbelief, leaving none a ground of merit, precisely that he might save all — both classes — by mercy alone. The verse displays the sublime conclusion that grace reigns over the whole, and breaks forth into the doxology of God's unsearchable wisdom (vv.33-36).
33:
"O the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God! how unsearchable are his judgments, and his ways past finding out!" The witnesses note the doxology of God's unsearchable wisdom. On "O the depth of the riches," the grammatical voices note the adoration. Barnes, Henry, and Robertson note Paul, having unfolded the whole plan of God's dealings with Jew and Gentile, bursts into adoring wonder at the depth of the riches of God's wisdom and knowledge — overwhelmed by the profundity of the divine counsels.

On "unsearchable... past finding out," the witnesses note the incomprehensibility. Barnes and Calvin note God's judgments are unsearchable (beyond investigation) and his ways past finding out (not to be traced out) — his counsels infinitely beyond human comprehension.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the posture: the proper response to the mysteries of election and mercy is not cavilling but adoration — bowing before the depth we cannot fathom. The verse displays the doxology of God's unsearchable wisdom.

The witnesses agree: Paul, having unfolded the whole plan of God's dealings with Jew and Gentile, bursts into adoring wonder at the depth of the riches of God's wisdom and knowledge (Barnes, Henry, Robertson) — overwhelmed by the profundity of the divine counsels, whose judgments are unsearchable and ways past finding out, infinitely beyond human comprehension (Barnes, Calvin). Henry and Calvin note the proper response to the mysteries of election and mercy is adoration, not cavilling. The verse displays the doxology. The verse marks the adoration: O the depth. The verse displays the riches: wisdom and knowledge. The verse displays the incomprehensibility: unsearchable, past finding out. The verse displays the posture: adoration. The doxology displays the adoring wonder befitting God's wisdom. The verse displays the wonder: at the depth. The verse displays the mystery: unsearchable judgments. The verse displays the response: worship.
