The doxology of God's unsearchable wisdom — O the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God — displays the adoring wonder that befits the contemplation of the divine counsels: having traced God's dealings with Jew and Gentile, Paul bows before the unfathomable depth of his wisdom, whose judgments are unsearchable and ways past finding out. The verse displays that the proper response to the mysteries of sovereignty and mercy is worship, and unfolds the grounds of this adoration (vv.34-36).
34:
"For who hath known the mind of the Lord? or who hath been his counsellor?" The witnesses note God's incomprehensible mind and self-sufficient counsel. On "who hath known the mind of the Lord?" the grammatical voices note the citation (Isaiah 40:13). Barnes and Robertson note the rhetorical question: no one has known (comprehended) the mind of the Lord — his thoughts are beyond all human understanding.

On "who hath been his counsellor?" the witnesses note the self-sufficiency. No one has advised or counselled God; he needs no instruction, having all wisdom in himself — his plans owe nothing to human counsel.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the humbling truth: since none can know God's mind or counsel him, men must not presume to judge or cavil at his ways, but humbly adore. The verse displays God's incomprehensible mind and self-sufficient counsel.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the rhetorical question (Isaiah 40:13) — no one has known (comprehended) the mind of the Lord, his thoughts beyond all human understanding (Barnes, Robertson); and no one has counselled God, who needs no instruction, having all wisdom in himself. Calvin and Theodoret note the humbling truth: since none can know God's mind or counsel him, men must not presume to judge his ways but humbly adore. The verse displays God's incomprehensible mind. The verse marks the question: who hath known? The verse displays the incomprehensibility: the mind of the Lord. The verse displays the self-sufficiency: no counsellor. The verse displays the lesson: humble adoration. God's incomprehensible mind displays the futility of judging his ways. The verse displays the mind: unknowable. The verse displays the counsel: needing none. The verse displays the humility: not to cavil. God's incomprehensible mind and self-sufficient counsel — who hath known the mind of the Lord, or who hath been his counsellor? — display the futility of presuming to judge his ways: since no one can comprehend God's thoughts or give him counsel, men must humbly adore rather than cavil. The verse displays the infinite superiority of God's wisdom over human understanding, and adds the truth of his independence (v.35).
35:
"Or who hath first given to him, and it shall be recompensed unto him again?" The brief verse notes God's absolute independence: he is debtor to none. The grammatical voices note the citation (Job 41:11). Barnes and Robertson note the question: who has first given anything to God, putting him in his debt, so as to claim a recompense? None has — God owes nothing to any creature.

On the meaning, the witnesses note the divine self-sufficiency. God receives nothing from man that he had not first given; no one can lay him under obligation. All that we have is from him; we give him only what is his own.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the bearing on grace: since God is debtor to none, salvation is wholly of his free mercy, not earned — no one can claim a reward as of right. The verse displays God's absolute independence: he is debtor to none.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the question (Job 41:11) — who has first given anything to God, putting him in his debt, so as to claim a recompense? None has; God owes nothing to any creature (Barnes, Robertson). God receives nothing from man that he had not first given; no one can lay him under obligation, all we have being from him. Calvin and Theodoret note the bearing on grace: since God is debtor to none, salvation is wholly of his free mercy, not earned. The verse displays God's absolute independence. The verse marks the question: who hath first given? The verse displays the independence: God debtor to none. The verse displays the self-sufficiency: receiving nothing first. The verse displays the bearing: grace, not merit. God's independence displays the freeness of grace. The verse displays the gift: none can give God first. The verse displays the obligation: none can lay on him. The verse displays the conclusion: salvation of mercy. God's absolute independence — who hath first given to him, that it should be recompensed? — displays that he is debtor to none: no one can put God in his debt or claim a reward as of right, for all we have is from him. The verse displays the freeness of grace, since salvation cannot be earned from a God who owes nothing, and rises to the final ascription of all things to God (v.36).
36:
"For of him, and through him, and to him, are all things: to whom be glory for ever. Amen." The witnesses note the great ascription: all things of, through, and to God. On "of him, and through him, and to him, are all things," the grammatical voices note the threefold relation. Barnes and Robertson note the comprehensive ascription: all things are of God (their source, origin), through him (their means, sustainer), and to him (their end, goal) — God the first cause, the upholder, and the final purpose of all.

On the threefold formula, the witnesses note the fullness. God is the origin from which, the agency by which, and the end for which all things exist — the all-encompassing relation of the creation to its Creator.

On "to whom be glory for ever," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the conclusion: since all is of, through, and to God, all glory is due to him alone forever — the fitting climax of the doxology and of the doctrinal portion of the epistle. The verse displays the great ascription: all things of, through, and to God, to whom be glory forever.

The witnesses agree the verse is the comprehensive ascription: all things are of God (their source), through him (their means), and to him (their end, Barnes, Robertson) — God the first cause, the upholder, and the final goal of all; the threefold formula expressing the all-encompassing relation of the creation to its Creator. Calvin and Theodoret note the conclusion: since all is of, through, and to God, all glory is due to him alone forever, the fitting climax of the doxology and of the whole doctrinal portion of the epistle. The verse displays the great ascription. The verse marks the threefold relation: of, through, to him. The verse displays the source: of him. The verse displays the means: through him. The verse displays the end: to him. The verse displays the glory: to God forever. The great ascription displays the all-encompassing relation of all things to God. The verse displays the origin: of him. The verse displays the agency: through him. The verse displays the goal: to him. The great ascription — of him, and through him, and to him, are all things, to whom be glory forever — displays the all-encompassing relation of the creation to its Creator: God is the source from which, the agency by which, and the end for which all things exist, so that all glory is due to him alone forever. The verse displays the sublime climax of the doxology and of the doctrinal portion of Romans, ascribing the whole of redemption and all things to the glory of God, and closes the great argument with worship.
